speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 1st 1996. This is an interview with liberator James Zuidema. The interviewer is David Klinger. We are in Muskegon, Michigan, the United States, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want those lights turned off for a minute? No, it's fine. OK, we're on. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is David Klinger. Last name is spelled K-L-I-N-G-E-R. This is an interview with Mr. James Zuidema in Muskegon, Michigan. Today is December the 1st, 1996. Mr. Zuidema is a liberator. Could you give us your full name please and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: James L, Lewis, Zuidema, Z-U-I-D-E-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and that is the name that you had at birth as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you were born on--

speaker: interviewee
text: February 2, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Muskegon, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your current age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about your childhood please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was brought up during the Depression. We went to St. Mary's School, graduated from St. Mary's School, all the way through. We lived about a block, or a mile, from downtown. And my dad worked at the dairy, so we always had something to-- a little to eat. A lot to drink, but not too much to-- you know. So we weren't too bad off during the Depression. He did work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four brothers. No, three brothers, and two sisters, and a foster brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were there any other family members living with your family at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had two uncles who were probably four or five years older than I was-- I'm the oldest one-- that lived with us. I never called them uncle because they were more like my brothers. One of them was killed at Anzio, and the other one was in the Air Force all through Africa and up through Italy till the end of the war. He just died a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you were in high school, you were aware that the war was going on. What was the feeling in high school at the time among students?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very patriotic. A lot of the kids were getting called out before they graduated. I had turned 18 just before I graduated, and I was trying to get into the Air Force, Air Cadets. My blood pressure was a little bit high, and I kept going back and forth to Detroit and finally they cut out the Cadets completely. And I was supposed to be drafted, and Sister Martin, the principal at St. Mary's, made such a stink about taking me before I graduated-- this was in probably maybe in March, early April-- that she got me a deferment until I graduated, and in doing so, she got a deferment for the whole city. Some of the kids left the next night, but I had about two weeks because I signed up for immediate induction, so it took them longer immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CHUCKLING] So you enlisted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well. I was inducted. I sort of enlisted, more or less. But everybody was gung-ho to go then. It was nothing like Vietnam or anything like that. Very patriotic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a lot of news of what was happening in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty much. The hardest thing for me was when [SOBBING] my uncle was killed, because he was so close to us. So I felt that I had to go right now, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad almost went the same time I did that. He was 20, just 20 years older than I am, but they gave him a deferment as an essential industry, which was the dairy. So he didn't go, but we almost went the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard anything of the persecutions that were going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some rumors, especially about the end of 1955 when the Russians liberated Auschwitz and Birkenau and a couple other places. But people, we didn't take it too seriously because after the First World War, there were a lot of rumors of atrocities, and they all proved to be false. It was just propaganda. I don't know if you're familiar with how they used to prop-- the Germans as the Huns and monsters and all that, and it was all false. So in World War II, they just figured, more or less, that it's just propaganda. The Russians were-- they had a real bad chip on their shoulder, and they had an axe to grind. So I don't think the people really believed it. That's why we didn't really believe it when we walked in that first day. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you enlisted on what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went in the army June 28, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Fort Sill, Oklahoma. Then I went directly, came home for 10 days, went to Columbia, South Carolina, and was sent directly to Fort Sill, Oklahoma. The 65th Division was already packed, ready to leave to go overseas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any specialized type of training?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had basic training on a 105 Howitzer, so when I got on the 65th, they fitted me on a wire crew, telephone. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know anything about that stuff. [CHUCKLING] When you were in training, did the Army mention anything about any concentration camps or anything that had been discovered by--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you did your basic training, and then you were assigned to the 65th--

speaker: interviewee
text: Infantry Division. Headquarters, Battery Division Artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when did you ship to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: New Year's Eve.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- Well, it had been '44 or '45, depending on what time of day you wanted-- it was January 1, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you shipped out from?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York? Do you remember the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but it was an Italian luxury liner. It was taken over by the Germans. And we had captured it, and we were using it as a troop ship. It was a big, like a big cruise ship. We were in a convoy of, I think, about 600 ships. In January the Northern Atlantic is very, very rough. All you saw was water, then you'd see sky, then water and sky. Everybody got sick. I lived on ketchup and bread until we landed. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who your commanding officer was?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, it was Captain Koschel. He just died about a month ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember if there were any Jewish soldiers in your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, we had-- Headquarters Battery DIVARTY wasn't a real big battery. It was more of a service to the headquarters staff, the general and the colonels and all that, but we had, I would say probably six or seven Jewish guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them spoke Yiddish or German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if they had heard anything about the atrocities or persecutions? Or was it an issue that was talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I say, there was talk, but nobody seemed to believe it. You know, rumors, like I mentioned, like in World War I everything proved out false. So people were getting letters. The people that got out of Germany, of course, didn't know what was going on once they'd left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I really, I don't know if the Jewish people really, you know, the guys in our outfit, knew what was going on or what. It's hard to say. We never really talked about that much. We were busy worrying about other things. [CHUCKLING] What were the concerns? What were people feeling as they were going across?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get the war over with. Flight, get it over with. There was a tremendous patriotic surge. When you stop and think about the way things were with Vietnam, the way people looked at the soldiers and stuff, it was entirely different in World War I or World War II. Everybody was in there doing their part. It was entirely different. And we couldn't wait to get over there and get in, chase the Germans away, you know? Make the world safe for democracy and stuff like that. We had a lot indoctrination stuff like that during our training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that continue on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much, everybody was too sick. [CHUCKLING] Including the officers and the general. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where did you land?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Le Havre. We were supposed to land in the northern part of England. That's where our advance detail was setting up camp for us, so we could unpack our stuff, you know? All our equipment had to be cleaned, cosmolined often so it wouldn't rust and stuff. And the submarines were too bad up in that area, so they ran us around the south end into Le Havre. And we went into what they call Camp Lucky Strike. All that was there was tents. There was no food, no heat. It was a mess. And our advance detail got there about an hour before we did, so you know what the conditions were. It was terrible. And the first thing they did was took our trucks and sent them up to the Bulge, you know, to carry supplies and stuff, because they were very short of trucks. We eventually got our trucks back. Otherwise we wouldn't have moved. But it was a cold winter. I was lucky. I was too young to know what to do, so they stuck me in the chateau as a runner between the two generals. And for a while at least, I stayed warm, although everybody else was freezing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to stay there? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. [LAUGHTER] But at least I had it a little bit, because for a while there, we didn't have any heat or anything. It was miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the front lines at that point in time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Front lines were-- they had just pushed the Bulge back, and they were still fighting in that area. They were back to where they were Christmas, when the Germans broke through in what they call the Bulge. And when we went into combat, we relieved the 26th Division near Saarlautern, which is Saarlouis now. It's in Metz, just east of Metz, in that area. It was this side of Saar River and this side of the Siegfried Line.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was more or less to the far eastern edge of France, all the way down, more or less. We hadn't crossed the Rhine yet, and we hadn't got to the Siegfried Line yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend at Le Havre?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Le Havre?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No time at all. We just got off the boat and went to the-- well, we were in Camp Lucky Strike a week or so, unpacking and stuff like that, getting our trucks back, because everything has got to be cleaned. Everything is covered with grease on the ship. Then we went directly into combat then. It took us about two days to drive from the west side of France, Le Havre, to the east side near Metz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter any civilians en route?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, all throughout the towns. They were-- they had been liberated already, the French, but they were very friendly to us. Very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period of time, did you hear any of the German propaganda broadcasts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have any radios.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe headquarters did, but the men didn't that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have access to any Army publications or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't neither.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were moving awfully fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you moved to the front lines at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which were in Eastern France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eastern France, near Saarlautern. It would be east of Metz, in that area-- Saarbrucken. Nancy is in that area somewhere. I don't know. We went north of Paris, through Verdun, Chateau-Thierry and that area, and then swung down. It's not too far from the Belgium border. I mean, not right on the border, but it's in that area where Belgium and France and Germany all kind of come together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall the date that you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we moved in there and 26th Division moved out. And they had been there practically all through the Bulge, since Christmas time, and it was more or less a stable situation. And then when they moved out, we started battering the Germans, and everybody on the line started moving. We crossed the Saar River, took Saarlautern and-- oh, I don't know how many different towns in there. We broke through the Siegfried Line. That stopped us about an hour, two hours. Didn't stop us hardly at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the date was then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, dates-- a day was the same, one day as-- no Sundays, no Mondays, just days. But then we crossed the Rhine. I personally crossed the Rhine at a little after 3:00 on Good Friday, because people were coming out of church from the Tre Ore service on Good Friday, so I knew exactly what time it was. [CHUCKLING] But then from then on, we just went-- we called it the rat race. We just made a bee line for Berlin. I was with Patton's Third Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And the Army was progressing fast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. The 65th Division at one time was on a spear head, and we were the closest division of any of the Allies to Berlin. We even made Ripley's Believe It or Not back in the States. They wrote us up in that column.

speaker: interviewer
text: For?

speaker: interviewee
text: For being the closest, and we were on a spearhead then. That's when they-- Patton-- not Patton-- Eisenhower sent down the order to stop. We weren't supposed to go any further. We had just had a-- we were-- let's see-- Erfurt, Ohrdruf is quite near Erfurt, not too far from Erfurt, and Erfurt is due south of Berlin, probably 100 miles, and we were north of Erfurt. We just had a big battle with a large group of SS troops, and it was quite a bloody battle. And we were starting on our way back to Berlin again, and that's when we got the orders. Normally, at this time, the Wehrmacht weren't doing anything. They may-- if you see a group of just ordinary soldiers, they might fire a few shots in the air and throw down their rifles and surrender, or they'd just turn away and walk away, so we didn't pay any attention to them. It's because a lot of times, we were out there all by ourselves. But we had to watch out for the SS. And then Eisenhower gave this order and stopped us and let the Russians go into Berlin and pulled us back to the-- it would be-- at the time we were probably 30, 40 miles northeast of Ohrdruf, and the Sixth Armored Division was about 30 miles southwest, on an angle like that, of Ohrdruf and they-- I heard all this afterwards. I didn't know it at the time-- but they got word that there was a large, underground communication center built in the Ohrdruf, in the town, and they wanted permission to go that extra few miles in to capture that communication center. Well, at the same time, we were coming back, and they were going this way. So we made a crossing maneuver, which is very usual for us. We were like, leapfrogging a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how we all happened to get in the same spot at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter many German civilians at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Not a whole lot, but the ones we did were very cooperative, very friendly, no problem whatsoever. We didn't run into any problem. And one time, I remember one time, it's beside the point but-- they didn't have me on the switchboard this night, and so they took me out on the-- when we're laying our wire, we'd lay wire from our advance headquarters back to our old headquarters. We kept jumping like this, day after day after day, and we would lay wire backwards. We'd go to our new headquarters and then lay wire back. That way, we knew where we were going. And they left me there in the middle of the night, cold, all by myself in this house with a telephone. The sergeant had a bottle of Goldwasser. It's a liquor with gold leaf, real gold, floating around in it. Some officer gave it to him, and he gave me that. He says, this will keep you warm. When they came back for me in the morning, I was sitting in the corner with my pistol, and my carbide, and an empty bottle. Oh, I was so scared. I was all out by myself. Germans all around us, but they were Wehrmacht, so we really didn't have too much to worry about. But if one of them walked into this house, I would have died. [CHUCKLING] But that's-- SS, we had to worry about, but not the Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take many prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we took quite a few. A while back, just before this all happened, we had liberated a French camp that had a lot of women in. I don't know what it was, but it wasn't like a labor camp. It was more like a PW camp, but there was an awful lot of women there for some reason. I don't-- I remember going in it, and that's all I remember about it. But I do know that we did liberate one French PW camp, but that was the camp. It must have been the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall where that was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was before this time, some place just west of Ohrdruf, in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there must have been 400 or 500. There were a lot. But they were in good condition, they didn't look like they're-- nothing like the slave labor camps that were found afterwards. But these were fairly-- I-- we didn't know much. We didn't know much about what was going on. You knew what was going on 10 feet away from you, but if it was 20 feet, you didn't really know what was going on. I just remember going in, you know, walking through and talking to these people-- not talking to them, you know, because we didn't speak their language, but going into the camp. I found out afterwards it was supposed to be a French PW camp, but why there were so many women there I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of French men as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were men, but there were a lot of women too. So I really don't know what the whole thing was about on that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here for a moment.
